speaker: interviewer
text: --is Dr. Brendon Phibbs, and the date is January 19, 1997. Dr. Phibbs, we left off-- on the last tape, you entered a bookstore in Dachau--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau. And the young Yugoslav, Titto Minor, wanted a book. So I took-- I had my carbine, or .45, or some kind of weapon with me, I remember, that day. Because we just took what we wanted, pretty much, in Germany at that point in the war. And we went in and I found-- I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Deutsch-English. I wanted a dictionary. He went and got it. And I said, that's militarily requisitioned for the inmates of Dachau. Signed. Before that had happened, however, when we first walked in, he-- this man was-- kind of an evil-looking little soul. Looked like Goebbels, the propaganda minister, I remember thinking, talking to some other Germans. And they were talking about, well, the goddamn fuss all these Americans are making. They out that camp, they got rid of some Jews. What are they making all this-- ja, ja, ja-- pure Nazi propaganda going on. But they almost purified the race, now they're going to liberate these Jews, that in there-- all the bad things about Jews, and the people in the camp. And it was obvious over-Nazi sentiments.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I let them talk and let them talk, and finally said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Greet God. That's how you say in Bavaria, like the day. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said-- [LAUGHS] I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] [GERMAN] would be security services. Of course, I wasn't, I was a doctor, but they didn't know that. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That was extremely interesting. Oh my god, they fell apart. Oh no, no, we don't-- we-- we were just a little people. We didn't understand--

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard the same lines over, and I told them what a bunch of damn liars they were. And I went and got the-- they gave them a short lecture in my spirited German. I wrote the receipt for the inhabitants of Dachau, gave it to the man who walked up. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The security boys will be home to see you. He was in terror. It didn't do anything except let me release some feeling, but it felt good. But this epitomized the Germans as a group, I must say. At least that generation. Maybe they don't know what the word guilt means.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you treated them nicely, they deserved it-- See? We didn't do anything bad. See how nice they're being to us. That was the kind of tortured logic they used. If you treated them badly, you were being mean. We are noble Germans, and we're being maltreated. It's a-- it's a funny mindset I saw in that country, believe me. I've never seen anything quite like it anywhere. We, in the Western world, have a healthy sense of guilt which we employ to keep ourselves relatively honest. But it's unknown. And the other thing is, if the government said it, it's right. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to German teenagers during the occupation. Of course, like teenagers anywhere, they were against-- against the establishment, as much as-- and the establishment having been Nazis, they would-- they were terrified to be, but they were subterraneally against it. So I explained Thoreau's essay on civil disobedience to them. And I'd say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Sometimes it is your duty not to obey. That was a shattering concept to these guys. That there's a higher right and wrong above any politician, Thoreau's message. And you'd better be ready to obey that. Or-- But by and large, I found it a pretty sick culture and mindset. They were pliable. They had been enthusiastic Nazis, enthusiastic racists. They went along very thoroughly with everything the Nazis prescribed. And there were brave Germans, but they were dead. The Nazis, I found when I put-- on my occupation time, they're talking-- had gone through every city, every block, every house. And anybody with any pretensions to being a liberal or anti-Nazi, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to having any opposition to their philosophy was eradicated. So I mean, that country was purged of all the decent elements house by house, block by block, and city by city. So a kind of swift, nasty Darwinian selection took place-- anti-Darwinian selection took place. The brave people, the conscientious people were killed or exiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever encounter any Allied POWs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many. And they were starved almost as badly as some of-- not as badly as the inmates of the concentration camp, but they were starved. The Germans were brutal to our Allied POWs. We'd liberated many of them, and they'd make them march 25 miles a day on almost no food. They were staggering, starving. And in the camps, we sent Red Cross packages to our prisoners, but only one out of 10 got that. The Germans took them. Just cold-bloodedly stole the Red Cross supplies supposed to go to our prisoners. One of my good friends-- who is now living in Casper, Wyoming-- he was a bombardier. Was shot down over Hungary and spent two years in a German prison camp. He's a short, thick-bodied man. But he told me when he went out, he could reach around his upper arm like that. Our prisoners-- the Germans treated our prisoners very, very badly. And there were well-authenticated accounts of-- another boy from the town I used to live in, Casper, Wyoming, was trying to-- during the final days of the war, prisoners were wandering around aimlessly. And this particular group were trying to get the American armies. And they were sleeping in a barn. And the SS surrounded the barn, set it on fire, and burned them to death. And machine-gunned the ones they-- That was their favorite trick at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if the Jewish-American or black American soldiers were treated differently than the other POWs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know that for a fact. I think many of the Jewish-American prisoners were-- were-- I am told, I don't know it firsthand-- were actually put in concentration camps and treated like-- they weren't treated as prisoners of war. But I don't know how common that was or how prevalent. I know it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever encounter any soldiers of other Allied forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the time. We were in the French First Army, so we had French. We get Algerians. We had Tunisians. We had the French Foreign Legion, believe it or not. We encountered the British.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any interactions you'd like to recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. And the Russians. And we got along very well. The interaction with the French consists of a lot of horse-trading with the Algerians. The boys with these long cloaks, you know, belts of ammunition over it, and turbans. And back in the February fighting. And they-- they're just, you know, they're great traders. And here are ignorant farm boys from Illinois. We start trading cigarettes for booze or something. [LAUGHS] And they got-- they came back wondering what happened to them. Because they would outfox them out of everything. But they're interesting people, friendly people. The French Regular Army people were stuffy in the extreme. We never understood them at all. When-- during the occupation, I was driving back through the French zone, which is down in the Black Forest. And I saw sign saying, [SPEAKING FRENCH]. There was great hunting in the German forests, and we'd shoot deer and stuff. This sign said hunting is reserved for general officers. That would have caused a mutiny in the American army, as you can well imagine that. If we saw a deer, we shot regardless of their signs, because they weren't about argue with the Americans. But the-- you could sense the old Vichy French type of mentality in the French Regular Army people. You can see where the French anti-Semitism had been so devastating in World War II, when they-- the Middle East, that the Vichy militia rounded up Jews and resistance people. You encountered that when you talked to them. We saw a lot of the British in England, of course, and they would occasionally [? lead us. ?] And we never had much contact with them, we were so far south. We saw lots of Yugoslavian liberated people. And lots of Russians, marching home, some of them. It was a-- we saw every race there was, every kind of soldier there was. It was an incredible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you worked during the occupation for how long, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just till November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned back to the United States when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States in November 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your rank at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Major.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you discharged, or did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was discharged. And-- Camp Grant, Illinois. And got it lined up-- I'd written back to my old chief of service at St. Luke's Hospital in Chicago and said, if I live through this, I'd like-- he said, I'd like to be a resident in medicine. Knew I wanted to take training in cardiology later. So I was a resident in internal medicine. Then because I had a lot of experience and I had an advanced degree in physiology besides my MD, they made me a research fellow my second year. And then I passed my specialty board, [? and then ?] I took training in cardiology in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came back and then you had a family? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Penny and I had our first two children in-- before I sailed overseas. Hank was born just before my division sailed. We had a frantic scramble to get them home to my father's house in Winnetka. I grabbed the plane, flew to Camp Barkeley, and got my division as we sailed for France-- for England, France. Then we had two more children afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the two born before I went over are Suzi, who was born in Camp Campbell. And Hank was born at Camp Barkeley-- Henry. He's an attorney now in Jackson Hole. And then after the war, over the years, we had two more children-- Judy and Hugh. We're typical Baby Boomers, a lot of kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you became a cardiologist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I taught at Northwestern and practiced in Chicago and the part-time faculty at Northwestern. And then because I'm an outdoors man and worked in Wyoming and liked the West, I didn't like commuting in Chicago very much. I looked around the northern Rockies, and those are-- the first multi-specialty clinic was getting started in the state there. They had a pediatrician from Harvard, an internist from Michigan, a physician from Columbia P and S, and so on. And that looked like fun. So I went out there in 1952, to Casper, Wyoming. And we-- there was only one other internist, he and I were partners. Slowly organized a heart lab, bit by bit, and by the time I got through, we had a-- the heart lab in the state of Wyoming. We had huge programs going for a prevention of rheumatic heart disease in schoolchildren, things like that. I wrote my first textbook on the cardiac arrhythmias. I was on the part-time faculty at the University of Colorado. I used to fly down-- after enough few years when I was well enough established-- every other Tuesday night, and spend Wednesday teaching at the university and sitting in on the conferences and so on. And spent 20 years there, very happy years. All the kids got raised and through college. And then-- I'd written two books by then and a number of papers, so people kind of knew I was. I let it be known in the academic circuit that I was interested in going to a full-time academic position. And Dr. Frank Marcus called me from Tucson and said, we're organizing a new medical school at the University of Arizona. Our university hospital opens next year. How would you like to join us and be in charge of electrocardiograph and the kind of thing I'd written about. So I'll make a long story short-- I moved here. And I've been here ever since, 25 years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in touch with any of the people you liberated at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way to do it. They-- it was just chaos in that point in Europe. We didn't know where they went, they didn't know where they were going. And some of them I'd love to talk to, and find out if they lived. But don't forget Europe was the mob of displaced people. Half the world was wandering around there in herds, in and out of this kind of camp, and that kind of camp, trying to get them relocated. And I can't imagine what's happened to them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what would you like to say to those who deny the occurrence of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is lunacy. It's like saying the sun doesn't come up in the east. It's something-- this is the best witnessed and best documented crime in the history of the Western world. Two million soldiers from the Allied armies-- I would guess, at a rough estimate-- walked through those camps and saw them. We all-- General Eisenhower saw them, General Patton saw them. Patton-- was a rough old bird-- was so upset, he vomited when he walked into one of the camps, Mauthausen or some place. The Germans kept marvelous documents with typical German thoroughness. They documented everything they did. There are war documented movies for the German Army Signal Corps of the executions by the sonderkommandos in the Eastern Front, when they lined people up by trenches and blew their brains out. There was no conceivable excuse for what we saw at Lundsberg. We saw it. I was there. I was-- anyone who denies it, probably they are Nazis. There were a lot of Nazis in this country before World War II. On the near north side of Chicago-- because I was studying German in college-- a couple of friends and I would go to these beer halls on a Saturday night to speak the dialect-- to speak colloquial German. They had Nazi flags. They were the DAWA, the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We hear them all talk about, by God, the race comes first, the nation comes second. Deep sympathies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The Milwaukee German beer families got Senator Joe McCarthy-- you remember General McCarthy, of the old fame? Well, the first thing he did before he started on his big Red hunt, when he was a young senator, he got the Nazi SS murderers of the Malmedy massacre liberated-- saved them from being hung. You know about the Malmedy massacre, when they murdered 200 American prisoners. And he raised a typical McCarthy uproar, [INAUDIBLE], and it was just enough so that the-- they didn't hang them. They went to jail, that was about all that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there's-- this kind of-- just as in France. France had the dry rot of anti-Semitism, reactionary royalism-- which almost wrecked that country, the Germans took advantage of it. And French-- I worked closely with the French underground-- I didn't have time to tell you that-- in-- in Alsace, because they're bilingual. And you'd sense this from them, that one reason France fell was it was rotten down the core with this kind of reactionary racial hatred that split it. You know, the Dreyfus affair. Well, there was some of that in this country, not as much. And the people today run around wearing Nazi armbands and young-- are doing the same kind of vicious, criminal, lunatic nonsense. They're trying to do-- and they're-- they're discrediting themselves when they do it. Because there can be no question about what happened. Too many million people saw it. Too many million people documented it. Any time they want to check with the German government, they have-- the films are available in archives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you keep in touch with anyone in your unit today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the time. We have an association called the 12th Armored Division Association. We have a meeting every year. And my particular friends in my unit, [? Bronx ?] [INAUDIBLE], nickname was [? Bronx, ?] signal officer, who had a hairy job, running around a light tank, trying to keep the guns talking to the infantry. Lives in Green Bay, and he spends Christmas with us many times. He's a bachelor. One of our most decorated officers, Lucky [? Lichens, ?] as he was called, [? Ellery ?] [? Lichens ?], who got two silver stars, lives up in Colorado Springs, and he come down here. And he went on to become a regular-- a colonel in the Regular Army. And as Liana says, he is the most polite man and also the toughest man she's ever met in his life. He won a Silver Star for calling down artillery fire on his own foxhole. Because the Germans were all around him, and he got on the radio and called it down. They said, but you're there, Lucky. He said, yeah, but I'm deeper than they are. Bring it in. And he just stopped the whole German attack. Some of them have died, of course. But we're very close. We stay in close-- very close touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have reunions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reunions every year. I go whenever I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a newsletter that comes out, with-- from your--

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a division newsletter, I have it right here. The 12th Armored Division Association News, you bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there one incident or memory that absolutely stands out in your mind during the time you spent in liberating Lundsberg and Dachau? Is-- if you-- if I were to ask you, is there one image that comes across your mind, do you know what that would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the man who died of CNS typhus. This poor, shriveled skeleton wracked by typhus, going into convulsions, bowing in this obscene arc. Thing we used to call a wrestler's bridge in college when I wrestled. So only the picture, the back of his head and his feet touching-- the rest jerked him like a-- like a bow string, you know, and then collapsing dead. And that was the epitome of everything these people did. All the German science, all the German industry, resulted in that poor, shriveled hulk. I remember thinking that as I stood by his side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken to any groups or children or students about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you bet. All the time. Everybody that will listen. And they're so well organized here in Tucson, I'm delighted to hear this. That I've talked to middle schools. I've talked to university. I talk to the medical students. I give a-- a basic physiology lecture every year, and I end up with about 15 minutes on the concentration camps. And the heroism of the Dutch physicians of the Royal Netherlands Medical Society, who saved at least 10,000 Jews by refusing to cooperate with the Germans at the risk of their necks, you know, at the risk of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your message to the students when you-- when you speak to them, when you lecture to them? What message do you want to give them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is that racial hatred is the thin edge of a knife that ends in horror. Once you admit it-- a little, at all-- you end up with Dachau. You end up with the destruction of everything you ever believed in. It is the worst germ that ever attacked the human body social, is what I try to get across to them. That you-- America's a multiracial society, one of the few in the world, if you think about. Multiethnic, multiracial. How do you define a race, you know, they argue. The anthropologists argue about it. But we know what we mean. Groups. People. Ethnicity. And we have every color and every creed in the world here. We-- we are unique in that way. And that to-- to admit the poison of racial, ethnic hatred, is the most un-American, subversive, traitorous thing they can do to the United States of America, is what I tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever written about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have my book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is the name of your book?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's published by my friends at Little, Brown and Company. Came out in 1983. And I'm happy to say it won the PEN West award for nonfiction that year. The Book of the Month Club alternate. It got around. I've had a couple of hateful letters from ex-Nazi-- from Nazi people, Nazi sympathizers in this country, after they read my book. Saying they just didn't believe the awful things I said about the Germans. And sending me stuff, anti-Holocaust literature, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you respond to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I couldn't stand to-- I didn't want to even think that such people existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message you would like, or advice you would like to give to the world, to-- to the future generations, before we end this interview? Is there any piece of advice, after having witnessed what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I'd think of an encyclopedia of advice. It's hard to come down to one thing. But that the irrational fractionating hatred of another person because they look differently, think differently, go to a different shaped church, go to-- eat different food. The idea of fearing and hating another because they're-- to use a simple word-- different, is the most destructive element in human experience. Has led to the wars, the massacres, and they're all irrational, and they have no basis in fact whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any war crimes trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I may-- one of my patients and friends in Winnetka was a lawyer in the Nuremberg Trials. But other than that-- I followed them very closely. My chief at Northwestern, Dr. [? Ivy, ?] the head of physiology, was on the committee that investigated medical atrocities. And he was a very famous man. I have a master's degree in physiology besides my MD, which I got by killing myself working while I went through medical school. And he talked to me at length about what he had seen. And he came back and we were visiting after the war. You know, the-- the maiming, the horrible experiments on pregnant women, the business of shaving a bone to see how thin they could make it be-- so they would still bear weight if you stood on it. Incredible maiming, torturing, and pointless experiments carried out by quacks. You know, they didn't even have the possible redeeming virtue that this was major science by major scientists. It was lunacy carried out by quacks. Not-- not only was it immoral in human terms, it was ridiculous in scientific terms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we end, is there anything else you would like to add, or anything you feel you've forgotten to say? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I don't think so. I'm just delighted you're doing this, let me add that. You know, we're-- I'm-- I'm 80, we're not going to be around that much longer in the course of nature. And please, listen to us, everybody. Please. Record this. Keep it. Get it out. And view it. Listen to us, that's the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for your interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the story ends, finally. After I was a widower for some seven years, I married a colleague, a hospital administrator, a refugee from Cuba, who had her own share of hardship in exile. My wife, Liana. Liana, look at the people and smile. For history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, this is a picture of me in one of our light tanks, which we often used to haul wounded. In this case, I'd bummed a ride as they came back from shooting up Germans across the Danube. You can see the empty shell cases on the-- on the tank. And this is a picture of me after a very cold night in the woods. I'd slept on that pile of hay the night before. And that's my old ambulance in the background. [INAUDIBLE] Here-- this is the entrance to the concentration camp at Lundsberg. You notice it says, danger of infection, entrance to the camp is forbidden. Even the Germans knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what we saw when we entered the camp-- masses of dead bodies so thick that it was hard to-- to walk anywhere without stepping on them. Sorry. And the faces, as you can see here, many of them were-- had been screaming just terminally-- you can see the mouths wide open, the shrunken faces, and the look of horror, especially on the third man in there. Here-- many more of the dead bodies we saw, partially clad. Strewn in every kind of disorder. And you notice the arms and legs here. That-- that man's thigh in the middle of the picture is about as thick around as you'd expect someone's forearm to be, but that's a human thigh, incredibly thin. Here we saw these burned A-frames. We didn't know what they were at first. Notice the A-frame, a lot of ashes in them. We found out that that's where their prisoners had been penned up by their guards when they had orders to kill as many as they could before the Americans arrived. They threw gasoline on, set fire to them, and you can see here, the bodies in one of the huts with the arms thrown up, obviously in agony. Charred, dead bodies all over the place. This is what they looked like. These were killed by 18 men of the German engineer group organization, [GERMAN]. And this is what they looked like. It was hard to imagine the scale of all this, even though this wasn't-- not one of the bigger camps. Here you see German civilians from the town of Lundsberg, who were-- marched out to carry the dead and give them decent burial, guarded by one of our soldiers, who's obviously enjoying making the Germans do this awful job. And you notice how well-fed and fit the Germans are, compared to the pathetic corpse that they're carrying. More of the same. On and on and on. Endlessly. Burned remains of dead people. And here's the burial that was organized by our division. You see the bodies laid out. And we had a rabbi, a Protestant minister, and a Catholic priest each read the services of the respective denominations. And then we had the Germans bury them. The least we could do for the poor people. And you see a German prisoner of war standing there in the middle of the picture, still in his uniform. This is the last picture I conclude with, this is a pretty sad-- terrifying commentary on what we saw at the end of World War II. Thanks. Leave it on.
